did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used computer vision and feature extraction techniques (asymmetry index, eccentricity, Hu's invariant moments, FFT normalization amplitude) combined with ROC/AUC analysis to classify benign nevi and malignant melanoma from non-dermoscopic skin lesion images. While it does not use deep learning, it employs computational modeling and automated feature selection for dermatological diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the investigation of two datasets (70 melanomas and 100 nevi), preprocessing of images, segmentation, extraction of specific features (asymmetry, eccentricity, Hu's moments, FFT), and performance evaluation using ROC/AUC metrics. The abstract explicitly states 'In this research, we aimed to identify and validate a set of relevant features,' and the structure includes Materials and Methods, Results, and Conclusions sections typical of primary research.

title: Feature Selection of Non-Dermoscopic Skin Lesion Images for Nevus and Melanoma Classification
brief_summary: This study aimed to identify and validate relevant features to distinguish between benign nevi and malignant melanoma using non-dermoscopic skin lesion images. The researchers investigated two datasets containing 70 melanomas and 100 nevi, applying preprocessing for noise removal and illumination correction before segmenting the lesions. Features extracted included asymmetry index, eccentricity, circularity, color distribution asymmetry, quadrant asymmetry, FFT normalization amplitude, and 6th and 7th Hu’s invariant moments. The relevance and discriminatory capability of these features were evaluated using Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves and Area Under the Curve (AUC) metrics. The results indicated that the asymmetry index and eccentricity, along with the F6 Hu’s invariant moment, provided good separation between malignant and benign lesions. Additionally, the FFT normalization amplitude showed potential for classification, suggesting its exploitation in future diagnostic tools.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two primary classes in the binary classification task. The study investigated 70 melanoma images from two datasets (D1 and D2). It is described as a form of skin cancer with aggressive development.

One of the two primary classes in the binary classification task. The study investigated 100 nevus images from two datasets (D1 and D2). It is described as a benign skin lesion due to the proliferation of melanocytes.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification between benign nevi and malignant melanoma
all_task_types.details: The paper aims to distinguish between benign nevi and malignant melanoma using non-dermoscopic skin lesion images. It evaluates features (asymmetry index, eccentricity, etc.) for their capability to differentiate between these two discrete diagnostic categories using ROC curves and AUC metrics. The study explicitly mentions 'classification' multiple times and reports accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity for class prediction.

datasets_used.name: Dataset D1
datasets_used.description: A collection of raw non-dermoscopic skin lesion images used for feature extraction and classification analysis.

use.training: Used for feature extraction and performance evaluation (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, AUC).
use.testing: Used for feature extraction and performance evaluation (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, AUC).
use.other: Served as the baseline raw image dataset for comparison with preprocessed images.

datasets_used.name: Dataset D2
datasets_used.description: A collection of non-dermoscopic skin lesion images preprocessed for noise removal and uneven illumination reduction.

use.training: Used for feature extraction and performance evaluation (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, AUC).
use.testing: Used for feature extraction and performance evaluation (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, AUC).
use.other: Served as the preprocessed image dataset to assess the impact of preprocessing on feature relevance.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the effectiveness and discriminatory capability of selected features in distinguishing between benign nevi and malignant melanoma.
To quantify the relevance of selected features and estimate their separation capability between classes; used to determine if features are irrelevant (AUC < 0.6).
To measure the proportion of actual positives (melanoma) correctly identified.
To measure the proportion of actual negatives (nevi) correctly identified.
To assess the overall correctness of the classification for each feature.
To compute the asymmetry of color distribution (Q) by comparing histograms of axially separated components of the lesion.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Feature relevance was determined using Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves and Area Under the Curve (AUC) metrics, with a heuristic threshold of AUC < 0.6 indicating irrelevant features.

Dataset D1 (Raw Images).description: Raw non-dermoscopic skin lesion images (70 melanomas, 100 nevi)
Dataset D1 (Raw Images).notes: AI and E showed very good/good separation. CIRC, Q, and ƛ showed modest separation. F6 showed good separation. F7 showed average separation. AI showed balanced accuracy for melanoma and nevi.

Dataset D2 (Preprocessed Images).description: Preprocessed non-dermoscopic skin lesion images with noise removal and uneven illumination reduction (70 melanomas, 100 nevi)
Dataset D2 (Preprocessed Images).notes: Better results were observed for processed images (D2) compared to raw images (D1). AI showed balanced accuracy. E and FFT showed high sensitivity but lower specificity. F6 had specificity/sensitivity of 0.82/0.72 with accuracy of 0.772. Q and ƛ showed no significant contribution. AI and E are identified as dominant uncorrelated features.

architecture_details.method_type: Traditional computer vision and feature extraction
architecture_details.description: The study does not use a deep learning architecture. It employs a pipeline of preprocessing (median de-noising filter, top-hat transformation for illumination correction), segmentation (Otsu's method), and feature extraction. Extracted features include asymmetry index, eccentricity, circularity, asymmetry of color distribution, quadrant asymmetry, FFT normalization amplitude, and 6th and 7th Hu’s invariant moments.
preprocessing_techniques: Top-hat transformation for uneven illumination correction

training_details.note: No model training was performed; features were evaluated using ROC curves and AUC metrics.

classification_or_output_head.evaluation_method: ROC curves and AUC metrics were used to assess feature relevance and discriminatory capability, not a trained classifier head.

preprocessing_techniques: Median de-noising filter for noise removal
Top-hat transformation for uneven illumination correction
conclusions: The asymmetry index (AI) and eccentricity, together with the F6 Hu’s invariant moment, are fairly competent in providing good separation between malignant melanoma and benign nevi.
The FFT normalization amplitude feature shows potential in classification and should be exploited as a descriptor of skin lesion malignancy based on geometric signatures.
The AI feature is competitive for multiclass classification of melanoma and nevus skin lesions.
Eccentricity and FFT normalization amplitude are relevant features for melanoma diagnosis.
F6 and F7 Hu’s invariant moments are appropriate for nevus diagnosis, with F6 being more highly recommended for classification tasks than F7.
The quadrant asymmetry (ƛ) feature failed to be a relevant feature for classification because analyzing lesions partially in quadrants causes loss of information.
The asymmetry of color distribution (Q) shows inferior performance compared to geometric features but has potential in malignancy evaluation when combined with geometric features.
Preprocessing images for noise removal and uneven illumination reduction (Dataset D2) yielded better results for all proposed features compared to raw images (Dataset D1).
The study aims to develop a teledermatology tool and migrate the method to a mobile application in the future.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small sample size (170 total images: 70 melanomas, 100 nevi) and lacks external validation, limiting generalizability.
The dataset consists of non-dermoscopic images, which may introduce bias compared to standard dermoscopic clinical practice.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; feature relevance was determined using a heuristic AUC threshold (< 0.6).
The paper acknowledges the need for benchmarking against other feature selection methods and studies on dermoscopic images, indicating a lack of comparative robustness evidence.
Preprocessing (noise removal and illumination correction) significantly improved performance, highlighting a dependency on image quality for feature efficacy.

The computational cost for some compared methods (e.g., Almaraz-Damian et al.) was noted as high, though the proposed method's computational complexity for Hu's moments was described as 'quite complicated'.
